speaker: interviewer
text: Leon Wells interview, tape six. Dr. Wells, you were telling me--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --about Kalwinski.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in any case, they kept me between the two. And they brought me over to this basement where they were hiding 21 other Jews. So we were now 23. And the idea was that from this time on my name became Baby. They did not know if the kids, [INAUDIBLE], the kids did not know that there is here three other kids. They did not know that there is English. Maybe it's my name, Baby, finish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did his wife and children know that he was hiding you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh sure, sure. Because they all took care of us. 23 people in a basement, you have to take care. And don't forget, during the war, it's even harder.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because if you buy food, what for do you need so much food? Somebody is seeing you have food. So they had to go outside Lwow to buy some food for us to feed us.

speaker: interviewee
text: They had their own stable, pig, and vegetables and so on. But, you know, during the war, the pigs have earrings with registered numbers. So if you kill a pig, normally, they discovered you that you killed the pig. Because you sold part of it. They caught you someplace selling it. But in our case, they killed a whole pig, and we ate it up. And it was really a sick pig, because there was no-- nobody could see anything from it. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did they get the money to feed 23 extra people?

speaker: interviewee
text: The other people had some money. And they themselves, as I said, we used up his little farm that he had. A gentleman's farm, a few pigs, a cow, you know, but we used it up in this.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in this way, I was hidden in this basement, which was very tight. Because it started with 6 people. It ended up with 23. And we had even to lay on one side, never to walk or anything. And the son that I gave you the other's who is in Gleiwitz in [INAUDIBLE], he used to sleep upstairs over the-- it's a stable over the basement. So in case in the night somebody hears somebody breathing, it was him or the pig. Because pigs breathe like human beings. So he was there so that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: There would be additional breathing noises?

speaker: interviewee
text: Addition-- [INAUDIBLE] breathing noises. And if he have-- if we turned on lights, he turned it on. You know, so there is some cover. So he slept right over us all winter, all year-round.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were there-- did they come around looking? And if so, who came looking to see if Jews were being hidden?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. They did not come to look for Jews to be hidden. Or, they did not lie. But one thing is the youngest Staszek, who was at this time eight, nine years old, 8 and 1/2 years old, he was a beloved child of the mother. He was the youngest and the baby. One day when the SS, the Wehrmacht, the German police were looking for houses, because the front became very close to Lwow, they move to Lwow. They-- he went over. And during the day, he walked towards his mother. And they looked over the stables. And he walked and said to his mother, look, look, the clock. The electric clock is running. We had the electric clock, like till today in America, running, one you use. But we in the basement had light. And the mother saw it. But the Wehrmacht did not pay any attention. But afterwards, they send away this kid to France, about 300 miles away. Because they were afraid he will talk out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were protecting not only you, but themselves.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they protected themselves. But it was tragic even the son. Because afterwards he was in United States [INAUDIBLE]. He was here studying. And it was a tragic situation for a kid to be sent to another farmer. They used him. He was crying every night, you know. And the whole idea for a 9-year-old, and 8 and 1/2-year-old kid for some Jews, for somebody else, not for me, you know, for your child, for somebody else. Because I want to save somebody or cares about somebody else.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was a very tense situation, like the tense situation which you [INAUDIBLE] nearby, a farmer, that had something to do with his 16, 17-year-old daughter, you know, a teenager. And he was hitting her or something. And she run out in the street. And she yelled out, you are hiding Jews and [? me to ?] you are against not giving me anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: Somebody heard her. And they came in, and they discovered Jews, 12 Jews. And they hanged him and his wife, the father. It was a girl of 16 years, a teenager, you know, running out in the street yelling, having a fight with her parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to those 12 Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Also, killed. And that was near us. So it was for us then, then them. But this is what can happen. It is not only the question if you want to help. It's-- you know, it can happen to anybody, a teenage daughter and so on. And I have the problem to people to understand this, you know, It's not only a question for yourself-- teenage daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed there from November till July 1944. And there, the Russians came in. And I was liberated. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you at the time of the liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the basement.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the-- And how did you find out that it was over?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- you know, everybody knew that-- the parents knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do the day of liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, the next morning we walked out the middle of-- before sunrise out of the basement.

speaker: interviewer
text: You hadn't been outdoors?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: We you able-- had you been able to walk around at all during your confinement?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But I will tell you, yes, that's a problem or what. But in any case, but the whole thing was that once you walk out, you can never come back. Because now everybody in the neighborhood will see that they were hiding Jews. They never-- nobody knew. Nobody ever knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to them, to the Kalwinski family?

speaker: interviewee
text: So this is what I'm saying. Kalwinski had-- in the morning before sunrise, you get out. And we don't see you anymore again, you know. And we all understood it. And we did not even think.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I walked out. And there's a description in the book, the affairs after liberate. I put on my shoe-- put on the first time shoes. And my feet started to bleed from the skin that start to rub off.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you-- you went to the street. You sat at the corner. And everybody start to look at you. And people say, he is a Jew sitting. And you sit there. And, again, this what I'm saying. The liberation was not something, big party or something and now you enjoy yourself and so on. [INAUDIBLE] And also, the whole world-- here's a Russian soldier. They come in. And you meet them. It is a different understanding.

speaker: interviewer
text: They say, why didn't you fight? Why didn't you go to partisans? You know, you cannot tell us you were in the basement while he was fighting at the-- on the front lines. You know, it's a different-- So when you-- when you got to Lwow, the soldiers--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --accosted you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not accosted, but they did not care about me. And [INAUDIBLE] I'm afraid to speak to them. They-- you were two different people and so on. And who cares about the 18 or 17-year-old boy? Let him go to fight. It was still not the war over.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened when--

speaker: interviewee
text: We had this hardship.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go back to your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went back to my house.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you find?

speaker: interviewee
text: People were living in these apartment. I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some neighbors. And I slept in my-- in a room where my parents slept for one night. And afterwards, I left, did not know where to do, what to do, and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your belongings still in the apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't think, no anything. I know my father someplace were hiding the silverware. I don't know, you know. It's like you say about your mother. They did not tell at this time, especially a kid of 14, 15 years, where and what and so on. It's a different. And we did not-- we were not of the rich people that had in Switzerland or what your read now. It's something not to my society. It was for somebody, you know, for a different group of people and so on. And so it is a difference. And so you sit. And they next day-- and it was interesting. So afterwards, when we found a lot of people that also survived, a group of people--

speaker: interviewer
text: You found other Lwow--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. And also a man, when I said about the sewer, he was hidden in the sewer with a group of people. And last week, I take a book to read about a black man with a white mother. And he speaks about Halina Wind in the last chapter. And that was a girl that I was in this, what we call, big apartment. She was around five years older than me, much more sophisticated. We had nothing to do with each other, you know. If you are age 17 or start the war at 14 and she's 19, they are more sophisticated. And she was also from more sophisticated family.

speaker: interviewer
text: So but and so and with her son who was in the we are very good friends. And we keep up till today. So he writes about the son in the all of this unknown to me. It is a very good book, A black man written. But it's a very-- so-- When you came--

speaker: interviewee
text: --in any case.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you came back to the apartment--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --where you had lived with your family several years before--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --you said there were other neighbors living there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: They recognized you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't-- they recognized me. I did not recognize them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ask what happened to your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did not need to ask. I knew. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I knew exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did-- how did the Poles who might have known you before the war, how did they react to the fact that here were some Jews who survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: They did not really react in any way. The reacted--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they welcome you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they surprised that you had survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were more surprised that I survived. But they did-- people did not welcome, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they offer any help?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. No, but they gave me food for the day when I stayed. They gave me a room. They gave me right away to wash up and so on. But it wasn't any really. We never had any relation with them. And they never had with us. And I did not recognize them. They must probably recognized me or not. I don't even know. I did not ask. I did not look. The next day, I left in any case this whole neighborhood and went to another neighborhood.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you stay in Lwow?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed in Lwow [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] when did I go to-- oh, yes. I stayed in Lwow about three, four months. Because afterwards, they had to move-- the-- the Poles moved from Lwow to the-- to the-- to Poland. Because this became Ukraine. The Russians took it over.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was that-- what was it like living under the Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had no problem, you know, these few months. And I moved out to Lwow-- to Poland, to Lodz. And I lived in Lodz and Gleiwitz. And I got an apartment in Gleiwitz that the Germans used to live. And they cleaned out the apartment and gave us apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who gave you the apartments?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Poles.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Pole.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you surviving and earning a living? Did you make any attempt to find any family relations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I was registered every place. And that was all. But how do you make any attempt to find somebody? If you're registered that you are from Lwow, you are registered in Lwow for four months and afterwards in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And also, because as my-- as I write in my book, it was the first book appearing. And everybody knew in Poland my name. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You know, it's like saying does Anne Frank need in Holland to tell that she's alive? And the Polish press carried that Leon Wells is a same as from Holland, Anne Frank. I was in the same situation, the same, [INAUDIBLE], and they same-- memories, the same. Tell me about the book--

speaker: interviewee
text: What--

speaker: interviewer
text: --and the circumstances under which you wrote it originally and how you got it published.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, in 1943, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I kept-- as I had these paper and pencils in the death brigade, somehow I kept scribbling like a lot of teenagers what I remember. Plus, somewhere, somehow, it was a very important part to be a witness to this Holocaust, to tell to the world what had really happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a sense of that as a teenager?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. It was-- this was repeated and repeated again by my grandfather, by my grandparents, my parents, that we have to be a witness. And that was even a part of our-- we are witness to this world, you know. And having been this idea of being a witness, we kept-- I kept scribbling what had happened. When I got into this basement in '43, I put it together and kept writing more and more, you know, to keep myself busy. The same as I-- in this basement, when I had light, I learned the whole prayer of the Catholic prayer catechism by heart. [NON-ENGLISH], the priests' prayers in Latin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because it keeps something to learn and this what it was there. You keep learning and repeating it, you know. Nothing-- for no other reason except to keep you mind-- to keep something repeating and learning. Nothing else.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were writing while you were in the basement.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yes. And there [INAUDIBLE]. And then I--

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you have it-- when did you hand it in to be published?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. In a while, I was liberated. I took it with me. And I carried it around. I did not have a home, [INAUDIBLE]. So I carried it, this manuscript that you see it here. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and in 19-- and I met in Lemberg they say about looking for somebody I registered and [INAUDIBLE]. I met my teacher from gymnasium, Mrs. [INAUDIBLE]. And she was a teacher. And her husband was my teacher in gymnasium.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I met-- and I-- and her husband was killed in Janowska camp. And she somehow, by-- by, I don't know, had some relation with this Dr. Philip Friedman, the historian that afterwards became a professor at Columbia University. And he saw me [INAUDIBLE]. And they were very much interested to what I write about, you know. Because her husband died in Janowska camp. So I gave him this-- this diary to read it. And that was all. And he became afterwards the head of the Historical Commission of Poland in Lodz. And the first thing what he did was he published some-- some originals of the war and books about the Holocaust war. And the first book that he published was this book, Janowska. Because [INAUDIBLE] and he with her had some relation and came in.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he married her. He needed, like, according to the Jewish law, two witnesses. They showed the husband was dead.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they asked me to be at witness. Fine, I never saw it, never. But I was with the death brigade. And he died in Janowska camp. So what could be a better witness?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because according to Jewish law you have to say, I saw him dead. And I buried him. And I saw him being buried. It's not enough to be dead. And here, I said I saw him being burned, being burned, finished. Couldn't be more witness. And so I was a witness for their wedding, with Dr. [INAUDIBLE]. And so they got married. So I became a part of this family one way or another, you know. I was everything that they needed. And they were everything that I needed.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, and I became a friend of Dr. Philip Friedman. I did not know him from before the war, because of this manuscript and the first book. So the first book published in Poland after the war about the Holocaust was my book. And that was published when?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was published beginning of '46, but really '45, right the first year. And you can see it from the paper that it was not--

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you leave--

speaker: interviewee
text: Lwow?

speaker: interviewer
text: --Lwow? When did you leave Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Poland. Poland. In 19-- you see, Mrs. Friedman-- was now Mrs. Friedman-- her sister was Doctor [INAUDIBLE], medical doctor. And she was married to Dr. Berg, who was in the Anders' Army in Italy. So she wanted to go to find her husband. She had to ask the army and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the fair thing was I will go with you. So I went with her to Austria. I sought him in the American zone in-- in Germany-- Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: --while she went to Italy. And she there met her husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you in Germany? And describe life there.

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in Germany, I went right away to go to the university and became a student. And when I finished in '49 and got my PhD, in meantime in '48 I applied to different countries. I did not have any relatives any place in the world. So any place that they said they have a scholarship for something, I took the test, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you support yourself in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: How I supported? I worked. I worked for the Joint Distribution Committee.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked. First of all, right when I came to Germany, having had the book published, everybody knew my name. So I right away became a part of the Jewish Historical Commission. So there I got a salary.

speaker: interviewee
text: Afterwards, I went to work for the Joint Distribution as a night watchman. And every night you get sleep here as a watchman. What do you watch there? [INAUDIBLE] warehouses, you know. So I sat there. And in the morning, I went back to school. So-- I support. Did you ever considered going to Palestine? Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In the beginning, yes. But I negated it for a very simple reason. Because I felt that they don't understand our Seder. Namely, when I became also a leader in the Jewish student body. There were about 500 students in Munich. And out of the 500 or so, we decided that we will go to Palestine at this time. And there was a law in Palestine, [INAUDIBLE]. It was very simple. That if you were-- you became a soldier. But if you were the second year of-- of university, you-- you had the right to finish the university. But if you start university, no.

speaker: interviewee
text: So at this time, I knew about this law. I asked the same people, in the Jewish agency, to give us the same-- the same if you are a third-year student, forget about it. But if you were first two years-- and I was only the first year. [INAUDIBLE] And they collected us all to go. And I said, no. Why? And I said, why? You have for your people. You have nobody. We-- our students have parents and so on. And I said, because we have nobody, we should be worse off than you people? So, but you cannot compare yourself to us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I came to the meeting [INAUDIBLE] in was at the newspaper [INAUDIBLE] and said, oh, no. We are not the same as you are. And we are not going to be the same. And this why I broke off over there. They even put it in [INAUDIBLE] and ever since. And even the-- one of the-- the head of the chemical, University of Jerusalem, the chemical department, is that he was with me but he had already signed. And I said, no. And this when I broke off for what they said, I applied to every place else, not to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about [? Rauch. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Right after the war, maybe '47, '46-- end of '46, '47, I was just in Germany, Munich. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] A friend of ours that was with me in the death brigade, one of the eight people that survived, saw him on a street car in Munich. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And he jumped on him. And they started to fight. So they were both arrested. You know, the whole idea was to get both arrested. And afterwards, he can [? start. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: So, and they called me right away also to recognize what it is. And he started the whole business. He's, by now, dead. And so it was. We'll stop right here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah

speaker: interviewer
text: But I will continue in a minute.
